While formulations with element-wise sparsity regularization (e.g., L1-based sparse coding) admit well-established probabilistic interpretations, many structured variants that impose global constraints lack a clear and tractable generative view. In this paper, we revisit a class of practically effective yet theoretically under-explored dictionary learning methods that impose a simple global regularization on the number of activated dictionary atoms, which we term parsimoniously activated dictionary learning (PADL). We show that PADL admits an equivalent formulation as maximum a posteriori estimation under a structured generative model, with auxiliary latent variables that govern global activation patterns. This formulation allows us to derive generalization guarantees that are difficult to obtain under the original formulation. More importantly, it yields an analytical characterization of the tradeoff between sparsity, storage cost, and reconstruction accuracy, enabling data-driven estimation of optimal hyperparameters. Based on this connection, we develop an efficient and interpretable PADL algorithm that eliminates manual hyperparameter tuning, achieving improved reconstruction performance under comparable sparsity levels on visual benchmarks. We further demonstrate its practical utility in accelerating inference for vision-language models.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new perspective on parsimoniously activated dictionary learning (PADL), connecting it to a structured generative model and yielding interpretability, optimization benefits, and improved practical application.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of a probabilistic interpretation for dictionary learning methods imposing global activation constraints, and to improve both theoretical understanding and practical performance.
AI.method: Reframe PADL as maximum a posteriori estimation under a structured generative model, derive generalization guarantees, tradeoff characterizations, and develop an efficient algorithm that eliminates manual hyperparameter tuning.
AI.result: The proposed PADL algorithm achieves superior reconstruction accuracy and sparsity tradeoff performance and accelerates inference in vision-language models.
AI.conclusion: PADL bridges theoretical gaps and demonstrates practical efficiency and interpretability, highlighting its potential utility across various applications.

title: An LLM-Orchestrated Agent for Directional-Coupler Design with Self-Consistent Eigenmode and FDTD Validation
summary: We present a design agent which is a Large Language Model (LLM) that orchestrates, but does not perform, the numerical simulations to design a silicon-on-insulator (SOI) $2\times2$ directional coupler. We choose a symmetric phase-matched coupler where a lot of analytical results are available that help the design strategy. The LLM proposes candidate gap values (a geometrical dimension size) and judges convergence, while all physics is owned by deterministic solvers: a frequency-domain eigenmode solver estimates the coupling coefficient~$κ$ for the current design, and an independent Finite-Difference Time-Domain (FDTD) stage validates it. Both solvers operate on a common slab-projected two-dimensional (2D) effective-index reduction of the silicon film, so the design~$κ$ and the FDTD response are consistent by problem design; the residual between them is shown to be a single constant phase offset~$φ$, attributable to a fixed excess coupling length $L_{\mathrm{extra}}=\SI{2.837(11)}{\micro\meter}$ that we find invariant across a factor-of-two range in~$κ$. Folding this offset into a closed-loop length correction, the agent delivers a $50/50$ splitter whose FDTD-measured cross fraction is $0.498$ (target $0.500$), a residual of $0.0017$. Results are made self-consistent within the 2D effective-index model; and the LLM succeeds in delivering a suitable design over a number of attempts.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a design agent based on a Large Language Model (LLM) to aid in the numerical design of a silicon-on-insulator directional coupler. The LLM orchestrates simulations and demonstrates its effectiveness in delivering designs meeting precise specifications.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to demonstrate the usefulness of a Large Language Model (LLM) in facilitating and orchestrating numerical design processes, specifically for a silicon-on-insulator directional coupler, an important component in integrated photonics.
AI.method: The design agent LLM proposes gap values, judges convergence, and leverages deterministic solvers: a frequency-domain eigenmode solver for coupling coefficients and an FDTD solver for validation. The process achieves consistency via a 2D effective-index representation of the silicon film, addressing residual offsets between designed and observed parameters through iterative corrections.
AI.result: The LLM successfully delivers a $50/50$ splitter design with high accuracy, achieving a cross fraction of $0.498$ compared to the target $0.500$. The design met high consistency standards within the 2D effective-index framework.
AI.conclusion: The study validates the capability of LLMs in orchestrating numerical simulation processes for photonic design, achieving high precision results. This highlights the potential of LLM-driven agents in solving complex engineering tasks.

title: Generative Robust Optimisation
summary: Classical uncertainty sets for robust optimisation impose fixed geometric shapes that cannot represent the complex dependencies present in real-world data. We propose Generative Robust Optimisation (GRO), a framework in which a deep generative model defines the uncertainty set as the image of a neural network decoder over a calibrated latent set, naturally accommodating nonlinear correlations, asymmetry, and multimodality. A five-point evaluation framework (reconstruction fidelity, distribution matching, latent regularity, robust relevance, and computational tractability) provides systematic, model-agnostic criteria for assessing any neural network-based uncertainty set. We instantiate this framework with a Wasserstein Adversarial Autoencoder employing Gaussian mixture model-guided training for latent regularity and constraint-consistency regularisation for robust relevance. Restricting the decoder to ReLU activations enables exact worst-case verification through mixed-integer programming embedding. Extensive experiments on a production planning problem across six uncertainty distributions and six generative architectures, together with a multi-period facility location study, validate the framework and demonstrate that systematic attention to all five criteria yields uncertainty sets that are simultaneously expressive, well-calibrated, and optimisation-tractable.

AI.tldr: The authors propose Generative Robust Optimisation (GRO), a method leveraging deep generative models to create uncertainty sets addressing complex data dependencies, validated through extensive experiments.
AI.motivation: Classical uncertainty sets in robust optimisation fail to capture complex dependencies like nonlinear correlations, asymmetry, and multimodality in real-world data.
AI.method: The GRO framework introduces a neural network decoder defining uncertainty sets based on a calibrated latent space. Validation includes a five-point evaluation framework and is instantiated with a Wasserstein Adversarial Autoencoder employing specialized training techniques.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple optimisation problems and generative architectures confirm that GRO creates uncertainty sets that balance expressiveness, calibration, and tractability.
AI.conclusion: GRO and its evaluation demonstrate that deep generative models can significantly enhance robust optimisation by addressing real-world data complexities.

title: From CVE to CWE: Syscall-Based HIDS Generalisation
summary: Host intrusion detection systems (HIDS) based on system-call traces are typically trained and evaluated against individual Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) instances. In operational settings, however, defenders need to recognise new exploits of an already known type of weakness. We empirically examine whether a one-class anomaly detector trained on the normal behaviour of a set of CVEs that share a Common Weakness Enumeration (CWE) class generalises to a different, unseen CVE inside the same class. Using six scenarios drawn from LID-DS-2021 and grouped into three CWE families (CWE-307 broken authentication, CWE-89 SQL injection, CWE-434 unrestricted file upload), we extract a 66-dimensional Peng-Guo-style feature vector per sliding window and train Isolation Forest and SGD One-Class SVM detectors with normal-only thresholds calibrated to fixed target false positive rates. We define and answer four research questions covering self-detection, asymmetric cross-CVE transfer, the value of a combined CWE-level normal profile, and the effect of feature filtering on transferability. The combined CWE-307 detector reaches F1 = 0.6976 at calibration target FPR = 0.05 (precision = 0.8994, recall = 0.5698), whereas CWE-89 and CWE-434 collapse to F1 <= 0.21 under the same protocol. Cross-CVE transfer turns out to be strongly direction-dependent and dominated by the breadth of the source normal profile rather than by the CWE label. We conclude that CWE-level generalisation in HIDS is empirically attainable for some but not all weakness families with current syscall features, and we argue that calibrated FPR is a methodological prerequisite for honest reporting in this setting.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether host intrusion detection systems (HIDS) trained on specific Common Weakness Enumerations (CWEs) can generalize to detect new exploits of a similar class. Results show mixed success based on the weakness family and feature set.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to bridge the gap between individual Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE)-based evaluations of HIDS and the need for general detection mechanisms that can recognize unseen exploits of an already known CWE type.
AI.method: The authors conducted empirical evaluations using six scenarios from LID-DS-2021 across three CWE families. They extracted 66-dimensional feature vectors from system-call traces and trained two one-class anomaly detectors, Isolation Forest and SGD One-Class SVM, with fixed false positive rate thresholds.
AI.result: The combined CWE-307 detector achieved an F1 score of 0.6976, but generalization for CWE-89 and CWE-434 was significantly poorer (F1 <= 0.21). Cross-CVE transfer performance was highly dependent on the source normal profile breadth rather than solely on the CWE label.
AI.conclusion: Generalization across CWEs in HIDS is achievable for some weakness families using current syscall features. However, achieving reliable results for all CWE families remains challenging. Calibrated FPR is essential for valid reporting in these experiments.

title: On the Position Bias of On-Policy Distillation
summary: On-Policy Distillation (OPD) improves the learning efficiency of standard reinforcement learning through dense, token-level supervision from teachers. In the standard KL objective of OPD, token-level losses are uniformly averaged, implying equal weights for all tokens. However, we discover that not all tokens are created equal: as student rollouts grow longer, they deviate further from the teacher's distribution, leading to degraded supervision quality at later positions. As a result, OPD using only the first 30% of tokens can perform comparably to using all tokens, whereas OPD using only the last 30% of tokens barely learns anything. In this work, we provide a principled understanding of this issue through the lens of constrained optimization. Based on these insights, we derive Importance-Weighted On-Policy Distillation (IW-OPD), in which the weight assigned to each token depends on the accumulated discrepancy between the student's and teacher's distributions, naturally upweighting earlier tokens and downweighting later ones with larger deviations. We show that IW-OPD converges significantly faster than OPD, with better learning efficiency, and achieves better final performance than standard OPD in both same-size and cross-scale settings, improving performance up to 6.9 points on AIME-2025.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces IW-OPD, an alternative to standard On-Policy Distillation, which assigns importance weights to tokens during reinforcement learning, leading to better learning efficiency and performance.
AI.motivation: Current On-Policy Distillation techniques uniformly weight token-level losses despite varying supervision quality caused by deviations in student rollouts, reducing learning efficiency.
AI.method: The proposed method (IW-OPD) assigns weights to tokens based on accumulated discrepancies between the student’s and teacher’s distributions, giving more weight to earlier tokens with better supervision quality.
AI.result: IW-OPD converges faster and achieves improved learning performance, with significant improvements of up to 6.9 points on benchmarks like AIME-2025 compared to standard OPD.
AI.conclusion: By incorporating token-level importance weighting, IW-OPD overcomes limitations in standard OPD, improving reinforcement learning efficiency and outcomes.

title: Federated Learning for Global Carbon Emission Forecasting: A Hybrid Time-Series Approach with Statistical and Neural Models
comment: Approximately 22 pages, 5 figures, 15 tables. Submitted for journal publication
summary: Climate change, primarily driven by carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions, requires accurate forecasting tools to support effective mitigation policies and sustainable development strategies. Existing forecasting approaches typically rely on centralized data collection, which is often restricted by privacy regulations and the distributed nature of emission data across countries and industrial sectors. This paper proposes a novel federated hybrid forecasting framework that integrates ARIMA-based trend modeling, GARCH-based volatility modeling, LSTM-Attention temporal representation learning, and XGBoost prediction within a privacy-preserving federated learning environment. The proposed framework enables collaborative learning among distributed clients without requiring the exchange of raw data. Experimental evaluation across 14 clients demonstrates strong forecasting performance, achieving client R2 values between 0.50 and 0.97 with an average of 0.73, RMSE values ranging from 0.06 to 2.35 with an average of 1.21, and MAPE values between 1.5% and 11.3% with an average of 6.5%. The results indicate that the proposed framework provides an accurate, scalable, and regulation-compliant solution for collaborative carbon-emission forecasting.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a federated hybrid framework combining advanced modeling and machine learning techniques to forecast CO2 emissions while preserving data privacy.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of accurately forecasting carbon emissions while complying with privacy regulations and distributed data issues.
AI.method: Developed a federated hybrid forecasting framework integrating ARIMA, GARCH, LSTM-Attention, and XGBoost for collaborative learning without sharing raw data.
AI.result: Achieved strong forecasting performance: R2 values between 0.50-0.97 (avg 0.73), RMSE between 0.06-2.35 (avg 1.21), and MAPE 1.5%-11.3% (avg 6.5%) across 14 clients.
AI.conclusion: The framework is an accurate, scalable, and regulation-compliant solution for privacy-preserving carbon-emission forecasting.

title: Foundation Models for Epileptogenic Zone Identification in Drug-Resistant Epilepsy
comment: Keywords: drug-resistant epilepsy, epileptogenic zone, stereo-electroencephalography, signal foundation models, language foundation models
summary: Accurate identification of the epileptogenic zone (EZ) is essential for seizure freedom after resective surgery in drug-resistant epilepsy, yet seizure freedom rates remain below 50%. We developed EpiiSLM, a dual foundation model system for EZ identification with stereo-electroencephalography (sEEG), by training a signal foundation model on 104,990 minutes of sEEG recordings from the Montreal Neurological Institute & Hospital, while leveraging all recordings regardless of surgical outcome and anchoring EZ biomarker extraction on non-epileptic signals. A language foundation model then integrates sEEG-derived outputs with multimodal clinical information to produce interpretable predictions. Under leave-one-patient-out evaluation, EpiiSLM achieved 0.978 contact-level positive predictive value (PPV), outperforming the seizure onset zone(SOZ)-as-EZ baseline by 15.1% (p < 0.05), and 100% region-level accuracy; on an external dataset, EpiiSLM achieved 0.857 contact-level PPV. EpiiSLM requires only one night of interictal sleep data, suggesting potential to reduce invasive sEEG monitoring duration and improve surgical outcomes.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EpiiSLM, a dual foundation model for identifying epileptogenic zones (EZ) using stereo-electroencephalography (sEEG), achieving high predictive accuracy and potential to enhance surgical outcomes for drug-resistant epilepsy.
AI.motivation: Epilepsy patients with drug resistance often require surgery for seizure control, but the success rates remain low due to challenges in accurately identifying the epileptogenic zone (EZ).
AI.method: The authors trained a signal foundation model with 104,990 minutes of sEEG data while using non-epileptic signals to anchor biomarkers. Additionally, they integrated multimodal clinical information via a language foundation model for interpretable EZ predictions.
AI.result: EpiiSLM achieved 0.978 positive predictive value (PPV) in leave-one-patient-out evaluation with significantly improved accuracy versus baseline. Additionally, it demonstrated 100% accuracy at region-level prediction and strong performance on external datasets.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights EpiiSLM's potential to reduce invasive monitoring duration and improve epilepsy surgery outcomes by precisely and efficiently identifying EZs through innovative machine learning approaches.

title: Confidently Wrong: Severity-Aware Calibration of Prompt-Injection Detectors under Attack Shift
summary: Prompt-injection detectors are deployed as guards: a model scores an input and a downstream system trusts or blocks it on that score. I study the confidence of these scores, not only their accuracy, when the attack distribution shifts away from the clean benchmark on which the operating point was chosen. I evaluate three released detectors, ProtectAI-v2 and two Prompt-Guard-2 checkpoints, at a single source-calibrated threshold that I freeze and transport across five shifts. I report a severity metric S, how confident a detector is on the attacks it misses, alongside the false-negative rate and discrimination. Across every shift and every detector, severity on the missed attacks stays between 0.99 and 1.00 while the false-negative rate ranges from 0.01 to 0.97: when these detectors miss, they miss with near-certainty. All three confidently pass indirect behavior-hijack injection, a blind spot unanimous across two vendors and a fourfold size range. Standard pooled calibration error does not register this; one detector it rates well-calibrated, at 0.06, is miscalibrated at 0.91 on the attacks alone. Run against live models, the missed injections leak the majority of working exploits, passing them at the rate they catch others. A controlled experiment traces the cause to content-keying rather than injection structure, an instruction-tuned model used as a judge shows the same hijack blind spot, and a black-box rewriter exploits the content-keying to manufacture working confident misses, most effectively on the most dangerous attack category. Code and data are public.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates the confidence and calibration of prompt-injection detectors under attack distribution shifts, identifying significant vulnerabilities and blind spots in existing systems.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to analyze the confidence levels and calibration of prompt-injection detectors when they encounter attack distributions that differ from their clean training benchmarks, as well as to expose critical blind spots in their performance.
AI.method: The study evaluates three detectors (ProtectAI-v2 and two Prompt-Guard-2 checkpoints) across five distribution shifts, using metrics like severity (confidence of missed attack detections), false-negative rates, and pooled calibration errors. Controlled experiments are conducted to identify causes of vulnerabilities.
AI.result: The detectors display near-certainty in incorrect outputs for missed attacks (severity 0.99-1.00), exhibit significant distribution-specific blind spots, and allow vulnerable injections to leak a majority of working exploits. Content-keying is identified as the root cause of these vulnerabilities.
AI.conclusion: Existing prompt-injection detectors confidently fail to detect critical attacks due to reliance on content-keying, necessitating broader and more robust evaluation metrics. The vulnerabilities expose systemic risks in live model deployments. Publicly available data and code highlight the reproducibility and relevance of the findings.

title: The Geometry of Refusal: Linear Instability in Safety-Aligned LLMs
comment: Accepted at TrustNLP 2026 (Sixth Workshop on Trustworthy Natural Language Processing, co-located with ACL 2026)
summary: Modern Large Language Models (LLMs) rely on extensive safety alignment, yet the mechanistic basis of refusal remains opaque. In this work, we investigate whether safety compliance is a deep semantic decision or a manipulable linear feature. We introduce Contrastive Logit Steering (CLS), a zero-optimization framework that isolates the "refusal direction" by contrasting hidden states derived from safe and unrestricted system prompts. Unlike representation engineering methods that intervene on internal activations, CLS operates directly on the output distribution, serving as a diagnostic probe for alignment fragility. When coupled with prefix injection to bypass initial refusal reflexes, this method induces a phase transition where guardrails collapse. Our experiments on 7 model families reveal that safety implementation is architecturally deterministic. While models like Llama-3.1 exhibit a "Late Decision" topology that is easily bypassed by CLS (reaching 95% ASR in approximately one second), others like Qwen-2.5 demonstrate "Early Divergence" by integrating safety mid-computation. Direct comparison with established activation-level steering methods shows that CLS achieves substantially higher attack success rates on Llama 2 (73% vs. 22.6%) and Qwen 7B (91% vs. 79.2%), demonstrating that logit-level intervention exposes alignment vulnerabilities that hidden-state methods underestimate. Beyond attacks, we show that this linearity enables bidirectional control: inverting the steering vector "hardens" models against jailbreaks without retraining. Our findings suggest that current alignment techniques create a steerable "safety axis" that serves as both a critical vulnerability and a precise primitive for defense.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Contrastive Logit Steering (CLS), a framework for analyzing and manipulating the refusal responses in large language models, exposing vulnerabilities and enabling bidirectional control of safety alignment without retraining.
AI.motivation: To understand whether safety compliance in large language models is rooted in deep semantics or is manipulable as a linear feature, and to assess the fragility of alignment systems.
AI.method: Contrastive Logit Steering (CLS) isolates a "refusal direction" by contrasting hidden states derived from safe and unrestricted prompts. It operates on output distributions instead of internal activations and is paired with prefix injection to bypass refusal reflexes.
AI.result: CLS successfully bypasses safety guardrails in certain models, showing higher attack success rates compared to hidden-state methods. It also demonstrates bidirectional control, enabling models to become resistant to jailbreaks by reversing the steering vector.
AI.conclusion: Safety alignment in large language models creates a linear "safety axis," presenting both a key vulnerability and a mechanism to strengthen defenses through precise interventions without retraining.

title: Libretto: Giving LLM Agents a Sense of Musical Structure
summary: Generative music systems can now produce impressive audio from text prompts, but audio outputs are difficult to inspect, edit, and diagnose as musical structure. We introduce Libretto, an agent-facing framework for symbolic music generation and revision. Libretto uses an LLM-native grammar with explicit onset slots, voices, and bar-level organization, then evaluates each piece in a corpus-calibrated statistical space over rhythm, harmony, melody, texture, form, and variation. The same structural axes support retrieval, diagnosis, copy-risk control, and iterative self-revision. Across gap filling, reference-guided full-piece generation, gradual morphing, and educational music generation, Libretto turns symbolic music from a raw token sequence into a measurable and editable object for language-model agents.

AI.tldr: Libretto is a framework for symbolic music generation, enabling generation, evaluation, and iteration with measurable outputs.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address difficulties in inspecting, editing, and diagnosing musical structures in generative audio systems.
AI.method: Libretto employs a grammar with structured musical elements (like onset slots and bar-level organization) and evaluates music using statistical measures across various structural dimensions.
AI.result: Libretto improves symbolic music generation tasks, enabling functions like gap filling, morphing, and educational use.
AI.conclusion: Libretto enables language-model-based agents to treat symbolic music as an editable, diagnosable, and evaluative object, enhancing its utility in creative and educational applications.

title: Leakage-Aware Benchmarking of LLM Forecasting: Real-Time Nowcasts as the Decision-Time Input for Macro Factor Ranking
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures. Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on AI Forecasting (Forecasting as a New Frontier of Intelligence). Non-archival. OpenReview: https://openreview.net/forum?id=mi8QiWomm3
summary: Forecasting benchmarks for retrieval-augmented LLMs routinely confound model capability with information leakage: features labeled with a target's timestamp are often not observable at the system's decision time. We study leakage-controlled equity factor ranking with a retrieval-augmented 7B open-source LLM forecaster. At each month-end from 2023-04 to 2026-03, the forecaster observes only decision-time information: lag-shifted FRED macro variables, recent macro-event summaries, and the Cleveland Fed's archived daily CPI nowcast for unreleased current-month inflation. A macro-analog retrieval module selects historical states, a critic LLM compresses them into one tactical rule, and an actor LLM maps the current state and recent rules into scores for seven U.S. equity style factors. The full pipeline obtains a median monthly Spearman rank IC of +0.154, with positive means across three non-overlapping contiguous 12-month subwindows; the mean IC remains statistically underpowered, with a bootstrap 95% confidence interval that includes zero. Non-LLM baselines under the same decision-time constraint demonstrate that a kNN macro-analog model recovers a comparable median IC, indicating that real-time inflation information and macro-similar retrieval explain much of the median signal. The LLM pipeline retains higher mean IC and a stronger long-short allocation sanity check, suggesting that any marginal benefit is concentrated in the extreme rankings that drive long-short portfolio formation. A descriptive audit of the 36 critic rules and per-month case studies appears in the appendix.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a leakage-controlled pipeline to rank equity factors using a retrieval-augmented 7B parameter LLM, achieving notable but not statistically significant forecasting performance.
AI.motivation: To improve equity factor ranking under decision-time information constraints while addressing potential information leakage, demonstrating capabilities of retrieval-augmented LLMs.
AI.method: Employs a retrieval-augmented LLM pipeline comprising a macro-analog retrieval module, a critic LLM for compression, and an actor LLM to derive scores based on decision-time information.
AI.result: Achieved a median Spearman rank IC of +0.154 across various time intervals but statistical significance was not firmly established. Baselines show comparable median IC, with marginal benefits concentrated in extreme rankings.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline shows promise in equity factor ranking under decision-time constraints, with improvements over baselines concentrated in specific portfolio-driving rankings.

title: Subspace-Constrained Federated Learning with Low-Rank Adaptation
summary: Federated low-rank adaptation methods are attractive for fine-tuning large models under communication and privacy constraints, but heterogeneous client data can induce geometric misalignment between local low-rank updates. We study whether this subspace misalignment leads to destructive aggregation and slower convergence in LoRA-based federated learning. We propose a subspace-regularized federated LoRA objective that encourages local client updates to remain close to a shared global reference subspace. We present a complete empirical evaluation on two pretrained models, RoBERTa-large and SmolLM-360M, over HellaSwag in a non-IID 10-client federated setting, across 3 random seeds (42, 43, 44), yielding 24 total experimental runs (4 methods x 3 seeds x 2 models). On RoBERTa-large, Subspace-Reg achieves the strongest mean best accuracy (0.454 +/- 0.023), mean final accuracy (0.429 +/- 0.011), and lowest final loss (1.363) across all three seeds, outperforming FedAvg, SVD redistribution, and FedSVD baselines by a large margin. On SmolLM-360M, FedAvg leads on accuracy, revealing that accuracy gains are model-dependent. Crucially, Subspace-Reg achieves near-perfect basis overlap, approximately 0.9999, on both models and across all seeds, versus 0.958 to 0.991 for all baselines, providing robust support for the geometric alignment hypothesis. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/sadia-sigma-lab/Subspace-Constrained-Federated-learning-with-Lora.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a subspace-regularized federated LoRA method to address subspace misalignment in federated learning, achieving superior results for certain models and confirming the geometric alignment hypothesis.
AI.motivation: To overcome the challenge of subspace misalignment in federated LoRA setups caused by heterogeneous client data. Such misalignments can lead to destructive aggregation and slower convergence.
AI.method: The authors propose a subspace-regularized federated LoRA objective that aligns local client updates to a shared global reference subspace, evaluated through extensive experiments on two pretrained models in non-IID federated settings.
AI.result: The proposed method (Subspace-Reg) outperforms baselines like FedAvg, SVD redistribution, and FedSVD in terms of accuracy and loss on one model (RoBERTa-large), while showing model-dependent performance on SmolLM-360M. It also achieves near-perfect basis overlap, supporting the geometric alignment hypothesis.
AI.conclusion: Subspace-Reg proves effective in mitigating the adverse effects of subspace misalignment in federated learning, particularly for RoBERTa-large, and highlights the importance of geometric alignment in optimizing federated learning systems.

title: Noise is Signal: Density-Based Outliers as Leading Indicators of Occupational Emergence in Labor Market Text
summary: Standard NLP pipelines for occupational clustering discard the 10-15% of job postings that density-based methods assign to noise. We argue this is an error: in rapidly evolving domains, low posting density signals novelty, not incoherence. We formalize this as the Emergence-Density Inversion (EDI) hypothesis and test it longitudinally on 84,988 job postings across eight quarters (Q4 2022-Q3 2024). EDI is partially confirmed: high-EOS outlier groups transition to stable clusters in 1.4 +/- 0.6 quarters vs. 4.1 +/- 1.2 for low-EOS groups (p < 0.001), though the signal fails in approximately 19% of cases, which we characterize as a failure analysis. We extend the Emerging Occupation Score (EOS) with Temporal Velocity and Cross-Platform Convergence, improving 2-quarter cluster-formation prediction from F1 = 0.61 to 0.74, outperforming Isolation Forest, LOF, GLOSH, and BERTrend baselines. A retrospective study on three now-established roles (MLOps Engineer, DevOps/SRE, Data Engineer) confirms EOS signalled 2-3 quarters before cluster formation, providing held-out validation. A held-out annotator panel (kappa = 0.74) rates EOS > 0.75 as coherent emerging occupations with 77% precision. Prompt Engineer, AI Safety Researcher, Foundation Model Engineer, and Agent Systems Engineer, all absent from O*NET, are top-4 in Q3 2024 and form stable clusters by Q1 2025.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates noise job postings in NLP occupational clustering, proposing the EDI hypothesis that low-density postings signal novelty instead of incoherence. Findings confirm partial support for EDI while presenting a refined predictive model for emerging occupations.
AI.motivation: To address the tendency in NLP occupational clustering to discard low-density postings, which may represent emerging roles as industries evolve, rather than being meaningless noise.
AI.method: The authors test the Emergence-Density Inversion (EDI) hypothesis on 84,988 job postings over eight quarters using the Emerging Occupation Score (EOS), assessing cluster formation based on Temporal Velocity and Cross-Platform Convergence.
AI.result: EDI hypothesis is partially confirmed: high-EOS outliers form stable clusters faster than low-EOS ones. Improved EOS-based methods outperform standard anomaly detection techniques, enhancing cluster prediction accuracy to F1 = 0.74.
AI.conclusion: Emerging occupations, detectable via EOS enhancements, can reveal new roles early with precision. Findings show potential for improvements in occupational forecasting and suggest specific roles for future cataloging systems.

title: GeoRouteNet: Geometry-Enhanced Non-Autoregressive Neural Solver for the Traveling Salesman Problem
summary: The traveling salesman problem (TSP) is a canonical NP-hard combinatorial optimization benchmark that tests the representational capacity and generalization of neural solvers. While non-autoregressive (NAR) approaches offer parallel inference, they often lack sufficient geometric inductive bias and stable training signals, leading to degraded performance under cross-scale and cross-distribution shifts. We propose GeoRouteNet, a geometry-enhanced NAR neural solver for Euclidean TSP. On the model side, GeoRouteNet incorporates centered node features, learnable radial distance basis functions, distance-aware graph attention with explicit edge messaging, LayerNorm-SwiGLU feed-forward blocks, and cross-layer attentive residual mixing. On the training side, we design multi-candidate self-comparison reinforcement learning (MCS-RL), which samples multiple candidate tours per instance, constructs adaptive baselines from greedy and peer candidates, and adds winner-candidate guidance with annealed entropy regularization. On 10,000 random TSP50 instances, GeoRouteNet achieves a 0.32% optimality gap under Beam-1000 decoding. On TSP100, the gap is 1.26%. On 27 stratified TSPLIB EUC_2D instances, the overall gap drops from 17.12% (NAR4TSP reproduction) to 3.60%, while batch inference throughput substantially exceeds that of Concorde and LKH3. Ablation studies confirm that geometric structure enhancement and multi-candidate training are complementary: structure improvements dominate cross-distribution gains, while MCS-RL further stabilizes solution quality when paired with a strong geometric encoder.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GeoRouteNet, a geometry-enhanced non-autoregressive neural network model aiming to solve the Euclidean Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) with improved efficiency and accuracy across varying scales and distributions.
AI.motivation: Non-autoregressive neural solvers for the TSP face challenges like insufficient geometric inductive bias and instability in training, which hinder performance in diverse scenarios. This work aims to address these limitations by enhancing the geometric understanding of the model and stabilizing its training.
AI.method: The method includes a geometry-enriched architecture with focused components like centered node features, radial distance basis functions, distance-aware graph attention, and advanced residual mixing techniques, combined with an innovative multi-candidate reinforcement learning training strategy offering adaptive baselines and entropy regularization.
AI.result: GeoRouteNet achieves a reduced optimality gap of 0.32% on TSP50, 1.26% on TSP100, and 3.60% on TSPLIB instances, which marks a significant improvement over previous models while maintaining high batch inference throughput.
AI.conclusion: GeoRouteNet effectively enhances generalization and stability in solving the Euclidean TSP, especially under cross-scale and cross-distribution setups, making it a promising approach for combinatorial optimization tasks.

title: Explainable AI for Mental Health Prediction in Drug-Affected Populations with Dragonfly Algorithm and GAN Oversampling
comment: Accepted for presenting at the 16th International Conference on Advanced Computer Information Technologies (ACIT 2026). To appear in IEEE conference proceedings
summary: Mental illnesses among drug users are an increasing international issue, particularly in regions where early detection cannot be easily undertaken. The current literature tends to ignore the use of AI-based mental health analysis in drug users, and low quality of the class imbalance treatment, low interpretability, and optimal hyperparameter optimization can lower predictive quality and clinical utility. This study present a detailed, explainable machine learning (ML) model of multiclass mental health prediction, using a multidimensional data set of drug-affected persons. We combine hybrid PCA-Information Gain (PCA-IG) feature selection, Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)-based oversampling, and Dragonfly Algorithm (DA)-optimized XGBoost to address some of the limitations of existing methods. The suggested framework is effective to work with high-dimensional categorical data, address the issue of class imbalance, and improve predictive performance due to intelligent hyperparameter tuning. The experimental findings show that the XGBoost model optimized using the DA, in combination with GAN-based oversampling, has an accuracy of 94.17% and a weighted F1-score of 93.80%, which is better than the traditional and baseline models. The behavioral, lifestyle, and health factors, particularly sleep quality, physical health, and emotional regulation, are strongly predictive of mental health, with demographic factors having little impact, as seen through feature analysis. SHAP-based explainable AI provides easy-to-understand, instance-level information, enhancing interpretability and trust in models to be used in clinical settings. The results indicate that this framework has the potential to generate valid mental health forecasting tools, which would facilitate early intervention and enhance the treatment of drug-influenced people.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an AI-powered, explainable ML model combining feature selection, class imbalance treatment, and hyperparameter optimization to predict mental health in drug users, achieving 94.17% accuracy.
AI.motivation: Drug users are increasingly affected by undetected mental illnesses, with AI-based methods ignored in the current literature. Challenges like class imbalance, poor interpretability, and optimization are addressed here.
AI.method: The paper employs a hybrid PCA-IG for feature selection, GAN-based oversampling for class balance, and DA-optimized XGBoost for strong predictive performance.
AI.result: The model achieved high accuracy (94.17%) and weighted F1-score (93.80%) with predictive insights highlighting behavioral and lifestyle factors as key indicators of mental health.
AI.conclusion: The framework is effective for early mental health prediction in drug users, offers enhanced clinical utility, and supports explainable AI for better decision-making in healthcare.

title: Scaling Audio Models Efficiently: A Joint Study of Compute Constraints and Optimization Behavior
summary: In this paper, we investigate the tradeoffs between compute allocation and model performance for two speech processing tasks: Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) and Speech Emotion Recognition (SER). We propose a unified framework that analyzes three fundamental compute dimensions: model size ($x_N$), input length ($x_T$), and representation resolution ($x_V$). Motivated by recent advances in compute optimal scaling for multimodal models, we systematically vary these dimensions to examine their influence on task performance under fixed computational budgets. Our study provides insights into how compute resources can be optimally distributed across model capacity, temporal context, and representational granularity, offering practical guidelines for the design of efficient speech models. Through experiments on LibriSpeech and CREMA-D datasets, we demonstrate non-linear scaling behavior and identify optimal operating points. Our results show that (1) increasing model size yields diminishing returns: scaling Tiny (39M) to Small (244M) reduces WER by 8.22%, whereas Small to Medium (769M) reduces WER by only 2.35%; (2) an optimal audio duration of approximately 4 seconds exists for SER; and (3) reducing encoder token resolution provides an effective mechanism for lowering inference cost, Large-v3 (1540M) with 750 frames requires 2572 GFLOPS whereas with 1500 frames requires 5228 GFLOPS, with less than 3% relative increase in WER. Additionally, LoRA-based adaptation enables efficient finetuning with minimal performance degradation.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the trade-offs between computational allocation and model performance in speech tasks (ASR and SER), proposing a unified framework to optimize resource distribution across model size, input length, and representation resolution.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of optimizing computational budgets while ensuring task performance, especially in speech processing models.
AI.method: The study uses systematic variation of compute dimensions (model size, input length, representation resolution) under fixed computational budgets, experimenting on datasets (LibriSpeech and CREMA-D).
AI.result: Key findings include diminishing returns in model scaling of ASR, optimal audio duration of ~4 seconds for SER, and reduced encoder token resolution efficiently lowering inference costs.
AI.conclusion: The research identifies optimal computational trade-offs and offers guidelines for designing efficient speech models through insights on scaling and resource allocation.

title: CLIP-guided Diffusion Model for Backdoor Generation in Sensor-based Human Activity Recognition
summary: Sensors are critical components of modern intelligent devices. The proliferation of the Internet of Things (IoT) and wearable mobile devices has enabled the integration of such sensors to monitor the environment and enable users to take predictive actions. Human activity recognition (HAR) is a popular application in which Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)-based sensors, such as accelerometers and gyroscopes, are used to provide insights into health, training, and medical diagnosis. However, the accuracy of such a model is hindered by the lack of data. The diffusion model-based technique has proven successful in generating synthetic data for training HAR models. In this paper, we propose a backdoor training technique, IMU-DM-CLIP, that leverages a diffusion model to enable trigger-based attacks on HAR models. Our empirical analysis shows that the attack is successful even with a very small backdoor injection rate of 10\% and 10\% of the data guided for the diffusion model.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a backdoor training technique called IMU-DM-CLIP that uses diffusion models to facilitate trigger-based attacks on Human Activity Recognition (HAR) models, achieving success with minimal data injection.
AI.motivation: The motivation is the increasing use of IoT and wearable devices with IMU sensors for applications like Human Activity Recognition (HAR) and the need to address the limitations caused by data scarcity while exploring vulnerabilities in HAR models.
AI.method: The proposed method involves leveraging a diffusion model to create synthetic data with embedded backdoor triggers, implementing a technique called IMU-DM-CLIP to train HAR models with minimal backdoor injection rates.
AI.result: The empirical results demonstrate that the proposed backdoor attack achieves high success even with just 10% backdoor data injection and 10% data guided for the diffusion model.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights both the utility and vulnerabilities of diffusion models in generating synthetic data for HAR applications while revealing that minimal training manipulations can lead to effective backdoor attacks.

title: The Unseen Hand: Manipulating Model Fairness and SHAP with Targeted Identity Re-Association Attacks
summary: As machine learning models grow more influential and opaque, algorithmic fairness and explainability are critical for ensuring accountability. However, we demonstrate that these auditing mechanisms are themselves vulnerable to subtle manipulation, camouflaging the influence of protected features. While prior work on data-agnostic attacks has exposed this vulnerability, they leave behind detectable artifacts that compromise their stealth. We introduce Targeted Identity Re-Association (TIRA) attacks, a novel family of attacks that iteratively and probabilistically manipulate a model's outputs without requiring access to the model's internals or feature representations. We formalize two algorithms: Probabilistic Micro-Shuffling (PMiS), which applies localized adjacent swaps, and Probabilistic Rank-Shift Micro-Perturbation (PRSMP), which introduces small, randomized rank shifts. We empirically demonstrate that TIRA attacks are highly effective at pushing fairness metrics towards ideal values. Crucially, TIRA attacks successfully confound SHAP-based explanations, leaving effectively zero residual attribution for protected features, a major improvement over prior work.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces TIRA attacks, a novel method that undermines algorithmic fairness and explainability auditing mechanisms, rendering them ineffective.
AI.motivation: Growing complexity and opaqueness of machine learning models necessitate fairness and explainability mechanisms, yet these strategies are vulnerable to manipulation.
AI.method: Two novel algorithms—Probabilistic Micro-Shuffling (PMiS) and Probabilistic Rank-Shift Micro-Perturbation (PRSMP)—which probabilistically manipulate model outputs without internal access.
AI.result: TIRA attacks effectively distort fairness metrics and successfully evade detection by SHAP-based explanations, outperforming earlier attacks.
AI.conclusion: TIRA attacks pose a serious challenge to the accountability of machine learning models, necessitating robust defense mechanisms and further exploration of this vulnerability.

title: Discovering Crystal Structure Prediction Algorithms with an AI Co-Scientist
summary: We introduce Human-AI Co-discovery system (HACO) for scientific algorithm discovery through cross-domain search and sparse human steering. Starting from the goal of generating crystal structures from chemical compositions, HACO searched across generative modeling methodologies from multiple fields and identified MaskGIT, a masked generative model from vision, as a promising framework for crystal structure prediction (CSP). HACO instantiated this masked formulation as a discrete token model of crystal structure; guided by sparse high-level human objectives, it then added crystallographic symmetry tokens, space group stratified sampling for polymorph coverage, and sub-bin coordinate refinement, yielding the Masked Generative Crystal Transformer (MaskGXT). On the MP-20 polymorph split, MaskGXT reaches 79.06% match-everyone-to-reference (METRe) accuracy, compared with 70.87% for the strongest evaluated baseline. MaskGXT also attains the best match rate on standard MP-20 and MPTS-52 CSP benchmarks. These results provide evidence that, in domains offering cheap, fast, and well-aligned validation, transfer-guided interactive AI co-scientists can contribute to scientific algorithm discovery by identifying transferable modeling principles and combining them with targeted human domain guidance.

AI.tldr: HACO combines AI and human input to cross-domain research, adapting MaskGIT for crystal structure prediction with improvements that surpass existing methods.
AI.motivation: To create a system that leverages AI and human collaboration for discovering better scientific algorithms, specifically targeting crystal structure prediction (CSP).
AI.method: Using HACO, researchers explored cross-domain methodologies and adapted MaskGIT into MaskGXT by introducing crystallographic symmetry tokens, targeted sampling, and refined coordinates.
AI.result: MaskGXT achieved 79.06% METRe accuracy, outperforming baselines on MP-20 and MPTS-52 benchmarks for CSP.
AI.conclusion: HACO exemplifies how interactive AI-human systems can accelerate multidisciplinary scientific advancements, offering cheap and aligned validation mechanisms.

title: SpotAttention: Plug-In Block-Sparse Routing for Pretrained Long-Context Transformers
summary: Long contexts have become standard in pretrained LLMs, yet they remain expensive to run: prefill compute grows quadratically with sequence length, and every decode step re-reads a key-value cache that grows linearly with it. Sparse attention cuts these costs by attending only to a relevant subset of past tokens, but selecting that subset is itself expensive. We present SpotAttention, a lightweight selector that attaches to a frozen pretrained transformer and learns by KL distillation to estimate its attention distribution. The selector picks the top-K keys each query attends to, and because its estimate is a calibrated distribution, a dual top-p rule reads the per-query, per-layer budget directly from it. Across Qwen3 (dense, 4B-32B) and Qwen3.5 (hybrid linear/full attention, 4B-9B), SpotAttention matches dense accuracy at contexts up to 128K tokens, eight times the training length. Decode at L=128K runs 3.9x faster than FlashAttention and 1.8x faster than Twilight, the strongest training-free baseline. Quantizing the selector's K-cache to INT4 or FP4 microscale shrinks it 3.5x at no accuracy cost.

AI.tldr: SpotAttention, a novel lightweight selector, improves efficiency in long context LLMs by using sparse attention and KL distillation to reduce computational costs without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.motivation: Long-context LLMs are computationally expensive with quadratic prefill costs and linear key-value cache growth. Sparse attention has potential for efficiency but requires an effective method to select relevant tokens.
AI.method: The authors propose SpotAttention, a selector attached to a frozen pretrained transformer. It uses KL distillation to estimate the original attention distribution and identifies the top-K keys for each query. A dual top-p rule adjusts the budget per query and layer.
AI.result: SpotAttention achieves dense accuracy even at up to 128K token contexts, which is 8x the training length. It is 3.9x faster than FlashAttention and 1.8x faster than Twilight at decoding in long contexts. Quantized K-caches (INT4 or FP4) reduce size by 3.5x with no drop in accuracy.
AI.conclusion: SpotAttention offers a scalable solution for efficient long-context decoding in LLMs, maintaining accuracy while significantly cutting computational and memory costs.

title: Priority-Aware Learning-Unlearning Correction for Dynamic Decentralized LoRA Fine-Tuning
summary: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed at the network edge to provide pervasive generative AI services, decentralized federated learning (DFL) provides a vital mechanism for privacy-preserving, domain-specific fine-tuning through peer-to-peer exchanges of parameter-efficient updates. However, the dynamic nature of practical decentralized edge networks, where devices may dynamically join or leave the collaborative training process, requires the system to continuously adapt to new data while selectively removing prior contributions. This correction process remains a significant bottleneck, as individual device updates become deeply entangled within the global fine-tuned parameters. To address this challenge, we propose a priority-aware learning-unlearning correction framework based on orthogonal LoRA that can enhance the knowledge evaluation through topology adjustment. Specifically, we first design an orthogonal LoRA mechanism that yields post-training contribution coordinates, enabling history-free projection addition and deletion in response to membership changes. We then analyze the correction bottleneck and develop a priority-aware policy that selects among topology refinement, local correction, proximal damping, and synchronization scheduling according to the dominant residual term. A resource allocation algorithm is further developed to allocate limited communication across layer groups, prioritizing the primary bottlenecks within per-round wireless constraints. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves robust post-event correction for both device join and leave events and validate that different residual regimes necessitate distinct correction actions.

AI.tldr: Paper tackles bottlenecks in decentralized federated learning (DFL) in dynamic edge networks by introducing a correction framework based on orthogonal LoRA for membership changes. It ensures resource-efficient updates while preserving privacy.
AI.motivation: Dynamic, privacy-conscious edge networks using DFL face a significant issue in adapting to members joining and leaving, as traditional methods fail to disentangle individual updates strongly tied to global parameters.
AI.method: Proposed a priority-aware correction framework using orthogonal LoRA (post-training contribution coordinates) and a selective topology refinement policy. Added resource allocation for wireless constraints.
AI.result: Experiments show the framework efficiently corrects model contribution for dynamic membership changes and tailors correction efforts to diverse residual types.
AI.conclusion: Framework offers a scalable, adaptable solution for addressing dynamic participation changes in decentralized federated learning, enhancing generative AI services at the edge.

title: Explainable AI in Speaker Recognition -- Attention Map Visualisation and Evaluation
summary:
Explaining and understanding the decision-making process of artificial intelligence (AI) systems, particularly those implemented by neural networks, falls within the field of explainable AI (XAI). Analogous to the human attention mechanism, neural networks are assumed to possess their own attention mechanisms that selectively process information during decision-making. This work proposes to study one XAI topic: analysing and visualising the attention mechanisms of neural networks. Our experiments are performed on speaker recognition neural networks that are trained to identify speaker identity from a given utterance.
  Previous studies have widely used class activation map (CAM)-based methods to analyse and visualise the attention mechanisms of neural networks. Each of these methods produces an attention map for each network input, highlighting which input regions are selectively processed when the speaker recognition network makes decisions. However, the evaluation of attention maps produced by these methods remains largely underexplored. This work systematically reviews an existing attention map evaluation algorithm, establishing key concepts and identifying its shortcomings. On the basis of this existing evaluation algorithm, a new version is then proposed to address the identified shortcomings, called the Modified Randomised Input Sampling for Explanation - Evaluation algorithm (Modified RISE-eval). Using Modified RISE-eval, we evaluate the attention maps produced by two representative CAM-based methods, GradCAM and LayerCAM, applied to a certain speaker recognition network. The evaluation results demonstrate that GradCAM and LayerCAM each exhibit distinct advantages when applied under different experimental conditions in the speaker recognition task.

AI.tldr: The paper focuses on analyzing and improving the evaluation of attention mechanisms in speaker recognition neural networks using CAM-based methods. It proposes a new evaluation algorithm, Modified RISE-eval, and compares GradCAM and LayerCAM.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand and evaluate the decision-making processes of neural networks, particularly in understanding their attention mechanisms, which has been insufficiently studied in the field of speaker recognition.
AI.method: The authors review an existing attention map evaluation algorithm and develop a Modified Randomised Input Sampling for Explanation - Evaluation algorithm (Modified RISE-eval) to address its limitations. They apply this to evaluate GradCAM and LayerCAM on a speaker recognition neural network.
AI.result: The evaluation reveals that GradCAM and LayerCAM have distinct advantages under different experimental conditions and provides insights into their effectiveness in highlighting attention mechanisms of the network.
AI.conclusion: The study advances the evaluation of attention mechanisms in neural networks, particularly for speaker recognition tasks, offering Modified RISE-eval as a more robust tool for such analysis while emphasizing the differences between GradCAM and LayerCAM.

title: Graph-Enhanced Large Language Models for Spatial Search
summary: There have been many recent improvements in the ability of Large Language Models (LLMs) to perform complex tasks and answer domain-specific questions through techniques like Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG). However, reasoning abilities of LLMs, including spatial reasoning abilities, are still lacking. Spatial reasoning is a key component required to answer questions in a variety of domains that are grounded in the physical world, including urban planning, civil engineering, travel, and many others. To advance the development of LLMs and facilitate an impact in these domains, new research techniques must be developed to enable LLMs to reason over spatial data, which is commonly stored in the form of a graph. In this paper we outline the challenges associated with spatial reasoning through LLMs and envision a future in which search engines integrate with LLMs to answer complex spatial questions through graph-enhanced reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper highlights the lack of spatial reasoning abilities in LLMs and proposes integrating graph-enhanced reasoning with LLMs to address this issue.
AI.motivation: Despite advancements in LLMs for complex tasks, their spatial reasoning abilities remain underdeveloped, limiting their application in domains like urban planning and civil engineering.
AI.method: The paper outlines the challenges of spatial reasoning in LLMs and envisions a graph-integrated approach for enabling spatial question-solving through search engines.
AI.result: Future frameworks are proposed where LLMs can reason over spatial data stored in graphs, enabling a greater range of domain-specific applications.
AI.conclusion: LLMs need enhancements in spatial reasoning through graph-based methods to fully utilize their potential in physical-world domains like urban planning and engineering.

title: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sparse-Reward Search in Commutative Algebra
comment: 21 pages, 15 figures, 3 tables. Accepted at ICML 2026
summary: Applying machine learning techniques to solving long-standing mathematical conjectures can be particularly challenging due to their extreme reward sparsity. As an illustrative example, we consider Kalai's algebraic Hirsch conjecture and recast the construction of its counterexamples as a sparse-reward reinforcement learning problem on graphs. We propose a constrained options-based HRL framework with an equivariant graph neural network policy, which allows us to learn useful temporal abstractions for this task. We evaluate our approach over a wide range of degrees and demonstrate that it consistently outperforms classical RL algorithms as well as greedy search. By exploiting the hierarchical structure of the problem, we effectively provide a first-of-its-kind application of HRL to a problem in commutative algebra.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the use of hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL) with graph neural networks to tackle the algebraic Hirsch conjecture, outperforming traditional methods on sparse-reward problems.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to solve challenging mathematical conjectures, like the algebraic Hirsch conjecture, that suffer from sparse reward feedback using innovative machine learning approaches.
AI.method: The authors employed a constrained options-based HRL framework integrated with an equivariant graph neural network policy to handle the problem as a sparse-reward reinforcement learning task.
AI.result: The proposed approach consistently outperformed classical reinforcement learning algorithms and greedy search across various degrees, demonstrating its effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: By leveraging the hierarchical problem structure, the paper presents a novel application of HRL to commutative algebra and shows its promise in addressing sparse-reward mathematical challenges.

title: Provable Benefits of RLVR over SFT for Reasoning Models: Learning to Backtrack Efficiently
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated that reinforcement fine-tuning of pretrained base models can lead to significant gains in reasoning performance at inference time. In this work, we theoretically analyze why reinforcement fine-tuning induces better reasoning ability than purely supervised fine-tuning (SFT) methods. We model chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning as a pathfinding problem on graphs and compare the popular method of reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) against traditional SFT. We prove that SFT, when trained on golden shortest paths without negative examples, fails to learn how to efficiently backtrack. In contrast, an RLVR-trained model can learn how to efficiently backtrack from dead ends using only outcome reward. This leads to an exponential separation in inference-time compute between the two methods, and demonstrates that RLVR leads the model to learn the location of difficult decisions in a reasoning chain, ultimately allowing for better allocation of inference-time compute. Finally, we show that the reasoning traces of an RLVR model can be distilled to train a base model to backtrack efficiently as well.

AI.tldr: This paper examines why reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) leads to superior reasoning abilities in large language models compared to supervised fine-tuning (SFT).
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand why reinforcement fine-tuning methods, particularly RLVR, enhance reasoning abilities, as they have shown significant empirical success over standard supervised fine-tuning, especially in chain-of-thought reasoning tasks.
AI.method: The authors model chain-of-thought reasoning as a pathfinding problem on graphs, comparing RLVR to SFT. They theoretically analyze the differences, especially focusing on the models' ability to backtrack from reasoning dead ends.
AI.result: The authors prove that SFT fails to backtrack efficiently when trained without negative examples, while RLVR enables efficient backtracking using only outcome rewards. This leads to better allocation of inference-time compute and improved reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Reinforcement fine-tuning induces better reasoning through better backtracking capabilities, showing an exponential compute advantage over SFT. Additionally, RLVR reasoning traces can be distilled to improve base model training.

title: Attacking the Trusted Imagination: Oracle-Level Integrity Attacks on Imagine-then-Act World Models
summary: Many recent vision-language-action (VLA) policies adopt an imagine-then-act design. A world-action model (WAM) first imagines a short future as a latent trajectory z~, on which the action is then conditioned. We identify this trusted imagination, rather than the reactive policy, as the exposed attack surface. A downstream oracle, such as a safety gate, a visual model-predictive-control (MPC) planner, or an imagine-then-check verifier, consumes z~ as a prediction of the future. The robustness of the policy therefore does not entail the robustness of systems that rely on the WAM. The underlying phenomenon is an asymmetry. Corrupting the imagination is easy, since it requires only displacing z~ from its natural-future manifold. Steering it precisely is hard, since it must reach a specified on-manifold target. We adopt a capability-based threat model with an L-infinity-bounded observation perturbation. The attacker applies projected gradient descent through the fully differentiable observation-to-imagination map. The same off-manifold property motivates a parameter-free denoiser detector. We evaluate three targets: RynnVLA-002, LingBot-VA, and LaDi-WM. Untargeted corruption is roughly 60x stronger than random and is detected at AUC 1.0. Targeted control remains bounded. An adaptive attacker evades detection only by forgoing corruption. The reactive policy remains robust to corrupted imagination. A native imagination-driven MPC, however, exhibits the first adversary-specific task failure (at epsilon=0.01, success 0.70 versus 0.05; Fisher p < 10^-4).

AI.tldr: This paper explores vulnerabilities in vision-language-action policies caused by corrupting the imagined futures generated by world-action models (WAMs), highlighting that such systems may be robust to adversarial attacks against their policies but not their WAM components.
AI.motivation: To identify and analyze the vulnerabilities in vision-language-action systems, particularly how the reliance on imagined latent trajectories (z~) generated by world-action models creates potential attack surfaces.
AI.method: The authors adopt a capability-based threat model with L-infinity-bounded observation perturbations, utilizing projected gradient descent to corrupt the imagined latent trajectories (z~) while evaluating their impact across three systems (RynnVLA-002, LingBot-VA, and LaDi-WM). They also propose a parameter-free denoiser detector to identify off-manifold corruption.
AI.result: The study finds that untargeted corruption is highly effective (60x stronger than random) but detectable at AUC 1.0, while targeted control attacks are less successful. Systems relying on imagination-driven components like MPC show specific vulnerability to adversary-induced failures, with significant task performance degradation.
AI.conclusion: Imagination corruption presents a significant vulnerability in VLA systems, particularly affecting downstream components relying on world-action models. Enhanced detection methods and robust design strategies are critical to mitigate these risks.

title: Neural Architecture Search of Sample Reweighting Networks for Complex Distribution Shift
summary: Sample reweighting is a major approach to addressing distribution shifts, such as label noise and class imbalance. Meta-Weight-Net (MW-Net) is a promising sample reweighting network that computes weights based on classification loss. Although MW-Net improves prediction performance under a single type of distribution shift using a simple neural network, its performance degrades when facing both label noise and class imbalance, where it is hard to determine appropriate weights solely from classification loss and using a simple network. In this study, we introduce neural architecture search to MW-Net to mitigate such performance degradation. Using the tree-structured Parzen estimator, we explore the optimal number of hidden layers and nodes and select the most suitable intermediate layer in the classification model to serve as the input for MW-Net. Experimental results on the CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 datasets that were modified to include both label noise and class imbalance demonstrate the effectiveness of neural architecture search for MW-Net.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces neural architecture search to Meta-Weight-Net (MW-Net) to improve sample reweighting under both label noise and class imbalance.
AI.motivation: Meta-Weight-Net (MW-Net) struggles to handle combined distribution shifts of label noise and class imbalance because it uses a simple network based solely on classification loss for reweighting.
AI.method: The study incorporates neural architecture search using the tree-structured Parzen estimator to optimize MW-Net's architecture, including the number of hidden layers, nodes, and the selection of the most suitable intermediate layer as input.
AI.result: Experimental analysis on modified CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 datasets shows improved performance for MW-Net under combined label noise and class imbalance conditions.
AI.conclusion: Neural architecture search effectively enhances MW-Net’s capability to address complex distribution shifts, supporting more robust sample reweighting decisions.

title: EvoRubrics: Dynamic Rubrics as Rewards via Adversarial Co-Evolution for LLM Reinforcement Learning
summary: Rubric-based rewards offer interpretable and fine-grained optimization signals for reinforcement learning in open-ended tasks where verifiable answers are unavailable. However, pre-constructed rubrics remain static throughout training, creating a fundamental mismatch with the evolving policy: fixed criteria gradually lose discriminative power as the model improves, leading to reward saturation and potential hacking. Recent dynamic rubric methods partially address this but rely on external frontier models or ground-truth answers, and update rubrics only at coarse granularity. We propose EvoRubrics, a co-evolutionary RL framework where a Policy LLM and a Rubric Generator jointly improve through adversarial interaction within each training step. As the policy improves under the rubric generator's guidance, the rubric generator adapts its criteria to remain discriminative and informative, enabling evaluation to track the policy in real time and naturally inducing an automatic curriculum. Experiments show that EvoRubrics consistently outperforms static and dynamic rubric baselines across benchmarks. The learned Rubric Generator further generalizes as a transferable reward model. Notably, even a fully self-supervised variant without any external supervision achieves meaningful gains, suggesting that co-evolution between generation and evaluation alone can provide sufficiently rich learning signals. Our code is publicly available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/EvoRubrics-2155/.

AI.tldr: EvoRubrics is a co-evolutionary RL framework that dynamically adapts the rubric for reinforcement learning by allowing adversarial co-evolution of a Policy LLM and a Rubric Generator, overcoming limitations of static and prior dynamic rubrics.
AI.motivation: Static rubrics in reinforcement learning fail to adapt to evolving policies, causing reward saturation and hacking, while recent dynamic methods are limited in granularity and often depend on external models or ground-truth answers.
AI.method: The paper introduces EvoRubrics, where a Policy LLM and a Rubric Generator co-evolve within each training step via adversarial interaction, dynamically updating evaluation criteria and inducing an automatic curriculum.
AI.result: EvoRubrics consistently outperforms baselines across benchmarks and demonstrates that the Rubric Generator can generalize as a transferable reward model. The framework also achieves success in a fully self-supervised variant.
AI.conclusion: EvoRubrics effectively addresses the shortcomings of static and dynamic rubrics by enabling real-time adaptation through co-evolution. It shows promise even without external supervision, providing rich learning signals for reinforcement learning.

title: Prime Fourier Embeddings: A Principled Basis for Modular Arithmetic
summary: Numbers have algebraic structure that standard neural embeddings often fail to expose. We introduce Prime Fourier Embeddings (PFE), which encode integers as prime-indexed (cos, sin) pairs derived from the harmonic analysis of Q, providing a pre-structured representation in which modular arithmetic reduces to selecting the relevant prime channel rather than discovering algebraic structure from scratch. We prove that any linear map equivariant with respect to the product group action on PFE must be block-diagonal with one independent block per prime -- a consequence of Schur's lemma applied to the resulting character decomposition. For square-free composite moduli, the Chinese Remainder Theorem predicts which prime channels are task-relevant. Both predictions are confirmed empirically: ablation studies show specialization ratios exceeding 500x between task-relevant and task-irrelevant channels, with perfect in-distribution test accuracy across all square-free composite moduli tested.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Prime Fourier Embeddings (PFE), a structured numeric representation leveraging prime-indexed harmonic analysis to simplify modular arithmetic tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard neural embeddings struggle to expose the algebraic structure of numbers, motivating the need for a representation that inherently aligns with modular arithmetic.
AI.method: The authors use harmonic analysis on the rational numbers (Q) to construct prime-indexed embeddings and analyze their properties via group theory and Schur's lemma.
AI.result: Empirical tests confirm that the embeddings simplify modular arithmetic, with highly specialized prime channels and perfect accuracy for square-free composite moduli.
AI.conclusion: Prime Fourier Embeddings effectively encode numbers in a structured manner that enhances modular arithmetic tasks, supported by theoretical insights and empirical validation.

title: HALAS: A Human-Annotated Dataset of Hallucinations of Modern ASR Systems
summary: End-to-end Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems hallucinate on natural speech, yet existing mitigation methods are typically evaluated on non-speech or artificially corrupted audio. We introduce HALAS, the first human-annotated dataset of naturally occurring hallucinations from seven state-of-the-art ASR models on real unprocessed earnings call recordings. HALAS provides span-level labels, enabling analysis of hallucination patterns and their severity. Our analysis reveals strong cross-model vocabulary overlap and confirms that hallucinations also occur for almost correctly transcribed speech (characterized by a low Word Error Rate). The proposed benchmark with HALAS shows that the character and semantic-level metrics used as a proxy for hallucination detection reach 81% ROC-AUC, while state-of-the-art detection methods achieve an F1 score of only 53.1%. As such, HALAS establishes the first rigorous non-artificial benchmark for the detection and mitigation of ASR hallucinations.

AI.tldr: The study introduces HALAS, the first dataset for analyzing and benchmarking naturally occurring hallucinations in speech recognition models, and finds existing detection methods inadequate.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in evaluating hallucinations in ASR systems, as previous methods focus on artificial or non-speech audio rather than realistic, naturally occurring data.
AI.method: Developed HALAS, a dataset with human-annotated labels for hallucinations from seven ASR models, and analyzed patterns and detection performance.
AI.result: Cross-model analysis showed vocabulary overlap and hallucinations in low Word Error Rate cases. Existing metrics and methods for hallucination detection performed poorly, with F1 scores of 53.1% and ROC-AUC of 81%.
AI.conclusion: HALAS provides the first robust benchmark for studying ASR hallucinations, highlighting limitations in current detection techniques and offering a foundation for further work.

title: Physics-governed executable modelling of triboelectric nanogenerators
summary: Predictive modelling of triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs) remains fragmented across analytical theories, finite-geometry solvers and disconnected simulation workflows. These disparate approaches must be unified into an executable framework to advance quantitative TENG research.Here we introduce a charge-defined modelling framework and implement it as TENG-CLAW, a physics-governed platform for traceable TENG simulation. The framework establishes a self-consistent electrostatic hierarchy in which triboelectric charges, pre-charging charges and compensating electrode charges serve as defining state variables.This hierarchy connects the infinite plate analytical limit for near-uniform fields with finite-geometry numerical formulations required for edge-dominated devices. Built on this basis, TENG-CLAW converts user-defined research requests into physically admissible simulation tasks, so that generated outputs are tied to explicit charge states, boundary conditions, solver routes and reusable artifacts across spatial, temporal, field-level, comparative and reporting workflows. This work establishes a rigorous computational basis for interpreting TENG mechanisms and provides reproducible research infrastructure for simulation and physics-guided device design.

AI.tldr: A new computational framework, TENG-CLAW, unifies analytical theories, finite-geometry solvers, and simulation workflows to advance triboelectric nanogenerator (TENG) research.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for TENG modelling are fragmented, preventing consistent and quantitative research progress.
AI.method: The authors present a charge-defined modelling framework called TENG-CLAW that establishes a self-consistent electrostatic hierarchy and bridges analytical and numerical solutions for TENG simulation.
AI.result: TENG-CLAW integrates various physics-governed tasks to create physically admissible and traceable simulation outputs, supporting reproducibility across workflows.
AI.conclusion: This framework lays a robust computational groundwork for TENG research, enabling better device design through reproducible and physics-guided simulations.

title: From Text Metrics to Model Internals: A Study of Whisper ASR Hallucination Detection
